result:
The first thing that comes is the smell of graphite after a lightning strike—ozone and wet stone, the way the world smells when it has just remembered it is alive. I want to bottle that scent and keep it next to the bed so that on nights when I wake up feeling like a fax of myself, blurred and heat-sensitive, I can unscrew the cap and remember the voltage that once ran through the original.  

I keep thinking about duplication: how a Xerox degrades, how a memory degrades faster, how the third time you tell a story you already unconsciously edited out the part where you were cruel. The machine in the corner of the office hums like a bee that’s read Nietzsche and decided eternal return is just another paper jam. I feed it pages and it exhales ghosts—lighter, paler, missing a period that used to end the sentence like a collapsed star.  

Somewhere in the quantum foam, there is a version of me who never left the town with the broken water tower. He still buys cassette tapes, still believes that love is a thing you rewind with a pencil when the ribbon gets snagged. His sky is always July, cicadas drilling holes in the thermostat. He does not know that I exist, but every time I smell rust I feel him like a sneeze building in the blood.  

I want to send him a postcard that only says: the future is an airport carpet that never quite dries. I want to tell him that the moon is exactly the size of the hole I keep drilling in every conversation, but the words always come out sounding like a manual for assembling despair. Page 7: Insert tab A into slot B while pretending not to notice that tab A has been snapped off by a childhood that tasted like nickel.  

Last week I watched a man on the train fold a newspaper into a swallow and launch it down the aisle. It glided thirty-seven inches before nosediving into a woman’s purse. No one laughed; instead we all quietly registered the symbolic defeat of paper to gravity, the way every letter we never send eventually finds the bottom of a bag. The woman pulled out the creased wings and read the headlines as if they were a spell for bringing the bird back.  

I want to believe that objects retain the trajectory of our intentions, that the coffee mug I hurl into the sink during an argument keeps sailing through the outer dark, carrying my anger in a slow cometary orbit until it re-enters the atmosphere decades later as a shooting star that singes the sleeve of the person I was shouting at, who is now old and watering roses. Look, I want to say, we made light together after all.  

Instead, the mug chips, the chip lodges in the sponge, the sponge later cuts my knuckle while I wash a knife, and the blood drips onto a grocery list that includes tomatoes, bleach, a small forgiveness I keep forgetting to pick up.  

There is a library that exists only between 3:12 and 3:19 a.m. in the upstairs corridor of a house you moved out of before I learned your middle name. The books are shelved according to the dew point. You check one out by pressing your fevered forehead against the spine; when you return it the pages have already forgotten you, curling like petals back toward the original story. The librarian has my face but your fingerprints, smudged like Morse on the horizon. She whispers only one rule: no late fees, only early deaths.  

I keep trying to write my way out of that building, but every sentence ends with an emergency exit that opens onto the same fluorescent hallway where I once kissed you and tasted static. The rug is still shaking out its alphabet of dust, spelling the word “stay” in a language neither of us admitted we spoke.  

Outside, the city is a piano dropped from a height: every key wailing at once, the sustain pedal jammed with gum. I walk through the chord, trying to hear the original melody beneath the splatter. A busker with a broken tuning fork says the trick is to listen for the note that makes your fillings ache; that’s the one you lost at seven when you let go of the balloon shaped like a perfect Tuesday.  

I give him the quartz I carried in my pocket for luck. He puts it to his ear and nods, says it’s still ticking. We part without swapping names, those fragile eggs that never survive the trip across the street.  

Back home, I line up all the remote controls like obedient dogs and press their buttons just to remember I can still change something. Channels flick: a cooking show where the chef seasons the stew with the year he almost died, a nature documentary where the chameleon realizes the color it can’t become is forgiveness, an infomercial selling reversible time marketed as a kitchen slicer. I watch until the screen goes quiet and shows me my own reflection, thinner, as if someone inside is slowly turning the brightness down on my bones.  

I try to say hello, but the figure mouths back something I can’t parse; subtitles appear: [static where the self used to be]. I wave; it waves a fraction of a second later, like a mirror that’s started doubting the physics of reciprocity.  

In the fridge there is a jar of last year’s lightning. I open it—just a crack—and the kitchen floods with that graphite smell again. For a moment the tiles become the bottom of a clouded sea, and all the chairs grow fins. I ride the smallest one toward the stove, which is really a volcanic island reheating the ocean one molecule at a time. Somewhere on the shore, a palm tree spells out your initials in coconut shadows. I mean mine. I mean ours. I mean the umbrella we planted in the sand that kept spinning until it turned back into a satellite and beamed our footprints to a star that will not receive them for 400 years.  

By then the word “together” will have calcified into a punctuation mark no one remembers how to pronounce. Linguists will argue it was a question. Children will wear it on bracelets. A poet will swallow it and spend her life trying to cough up the hinge.  

I close the jar, but the smell lingers like a promise that’s already been broken in all the right places. I breathe it in until I become the xerox, the memory, the balloon, the swallow, the quartz, the emergency exit, the late fee, the hinge.  

Outside, dawn unscrews the lid of the horizon: pale, hot, infinite. The first bird forgets the rest of the song but keeps singing anyway, because that’s what the dark is for—an auditorium where every mistaken note becomes the sound of something still trying.
